topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the dull thud of something heavy falling onto a desk. "Due to unforeseen circumstances, the surprise party for Sarah has been cancelled." I reread the words three times, the fluorescent office light buzzing overhead suddenly sounding deafening. A tight knot formed in my stomach, twisting low in my abdomen. I didn't even bother clicking 'reply.' Instead, I leaned back in my chair, the leather groaning in protest, and stared blankly at the wall. Every perfectly coordinated decoration, every whispered secret planned over the last six weeks, evaporated into a thin, acrid smell of disappointment. My jaw tightened, and I picked up my pen, but the ink seemed to swim before it touched the paper.

[story 2]

I watched the group chat, my thumb hovering over the screen, a slow, cold burn spreading across my chest. The silence in the room was thick enough to choke on. When Mark finally spoke, his voice was too bright, too cheerful, and it grated on my nerves like sandpaper. "Well, that's a bummer," he chirped, trying to inject some forced lightness into the atmosphere. I didn't look up. I focused instead on the way the afternoon sun caught the dust motes dancing in the air—each speck mocking the grand scale of what had been planned. I felt a distinct hardening around my eyes, a refusal to acknowledge the cheerful facade anyone else was trying to maintain. I simply took a slow, deliberate sip of my lukewarm coffee, letting the bitterness settle in my throat.

[story 3]

It was the sheer absurdity of the cancellation that made my hands shake slightly. I walked over to the window, pressing my forehead against the cool glass. Outside, the city continued its relentless, indifferent motion, utterly unconcerned with the delicate structure of my personal celebrations. I could almost hear the muffled, strained laughter from the planning group, a sound that felt miles away, belonging to a reality I was no longer part of. A low, involuntary sound escaped my throat—a sharp exhale that held no warmth. I turned away from the view, the movement stiff and jerky, and began methodically stacking the files on my desk, each folder placed with unnecessary, aggressive precision.
stories: The email landed in my inbox with the dull thud of something heavy falling onto a desk. "Due to unforeseen circumstances, the surprise party for Sarah has been cancelled." I reread the words three times, the fluorescent office light buzzing overhead suddenly sounding deafening. A tight knot formed in my stomach, twisting low in my abdomen. I didn't even bother clicking 'reply.' Instead, I leaned back in my chair, the leather groaning in protest, and stared blankly at the wall. Every perfectly coordinated decoration, every whispered secret planned over the last six weeks, evaporated into a thin, acrid smell of disappointment. My jaw tightened, and I picked up my pen, but the ink seemed to swim before it touched the paper.

I watched the group chat, my thumb hovering over the screen, a slow, cold burn spreading across my chest. The silence in the room was thick enough to choke on. When Mark finally spoke, his voice was too bright, too cheerful, and it grated on my nerves like sandpaper. "Well, that's a bummer," he chirped, trying to inject some forced lightness into the atmosphere. I didn't look up. I focused instead on the way the afternoon sun caught the dust motes dancing in the air—each speck mocking the grand scale of what had been planned. I felt a distinct hardening around my eyes, a refusal to acknowledge the cheerful facade anyone else was trying to maintain. I simply took a slow, deliberate sip of my lukewarm coffee, letting the bitterness settle in my throat.

It was the sheer absurdity of the cancellation that made my hands shake slightly. I walked over to the window, pressing my forehead against the cool glass. Outside, the city continued its relentless, indifferent motion, utterly unconcerned with the delicate structure of my personal celebrations. I could almost hear the muffled, strained laughter from the planning group, a sound that felt miles away, belonging to a reality I was no longer part of. A low, involuntary sound escaped my throat—a sharp exhale that held no warmth. I turned away from the view, the movement stiff and jerky, and began methodically stacking the files on my desk, each folder placed with unnecessary, aggressive precision.
